summary:
Averroes (Ibn Rushd, 1126–1198 AD) was the greatest Islamic philosopher of the medieval period and the most influential interpreter of Aristotle in the Western tradition, a Muslim jurist, physician, and philosopher from Córdoba in Al-Andalus (Islamic Spain) whose commentaries on Aristotle were so comprehensive and authoritative that Latin scholastic philosophers simply called him 'The Commentator' — Aristotle being 'The Philosopher.' Born into a family of distinguished jurists who served the Almohad dynasty, Averroes produced detailed commentaries on virtually all of Aristotle's works, arguing for the primacy of reason and demonstrating that Aristotelian philosophy was compatible with (and indeed necessary for understanding) Islamic theology, while also developing his own distinctive positions on the eternity of the world, the unity of the intellect, and the relationship between philosophy and religious law.

Averroes' impact on Western intellectual history was profound and paradoxical: his works, translated into Latin by Michael Scot and others in the 12th–13th centuries, sparked a revolution in European philosophy, providing Scholastic thinkers — most famously [[event:2293|Thomas Aquinas]] — with the full corpus of Aristotle's philosophy and the tools to integrate it with Christian theology. Yet in the Islamic world, his influence was far more limited: he fell from Almohad favor late in life, his philosophical works were publicly burned, and he spent time in exile before rehabilitation. Averroism — the school of Latin Averroists at the University of Paris who took his interpretation of Aristotle to radical conclusions, including the denial of personal immortality — was explicitly condemned by the Church, yet his transmission of Aristotle remained foundational for the entire European university tradition. Averroes also produced an important medical encyclopedia (the Kulliyyat, known in Latin as Colliget) and significant works on Islamic jurisprudence.
